name: Application of Derivatives
subject: Maths

sections.heading: 6.1 Rate of Change
sections.body:
If $y=f(x)$ then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ measures the instantaneous rate of change of $y$ with respect to $x$. When two variables both depend on time $t$, related rates link their derivatives via the chain rule.

Strategy. Identify the geometric/physical relationship; differentiate w.r.t. $t$ implicitly; substitute the given instantaneous values to find the unknown rate.

Examples: $V=\tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}\Rightarrow \dfrac{dV}{dt}=4\pi r^{2}\dfrac{dr}{dt}$; for a square of side $x$, $\dfrac{dA}{dt}=2x\dfrac{dx}{dt}$.

sections.heading: 6.2 Increasing & Decreasing Functions
sections.body:
$f$ is increasing on $I$ if $x_1

First-derivative test. Let $f$ be continuous on $[a,b]$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$.

  • $f'(x)>0\;\forall x\in(a,b)\Rightarrow f$ strictly increasing on $[a,b]$.
  • $f'(x)<0\;\forall x\in(a,b)\Rightarrow f$ strictly decreasing on $[a,b]$.
  • $f'(x)=0\;\forall x\in(a,b)\Rightarrow f$ constant on $[a,b]$.

sections.heading: 6.3 Tangents and Normals
sections.body:
Slope of tangent to $y=f(x)$ at $(x_0,y_0)$: $m=f'(x_0)$.

Tangent line: $y-y_0=f'(x_0)(x-x_0)$.

Normal line (perpendicular to tangent): slope $=-1/f'(x_0)$, equation $y-y_0=-\dfrac{1}{f'(x_0)}(x-x_0)$.

Special cases: vertical tangent if $f'(x_0)$ infinite; horizontal tangent if $f'(x_0)=0$.

sections.heading: 6.4 Approximations & Differentials
sections.body:
For small $\Delta x$, $f(x+\Delta x)\approx f(x)+f'(x)\Delta x.$

Equivalently, $dy=f'(x)\,dx$ where $dy$ is the differential of $y$ and $dx=\Delta x$. The approximate change in $y$ is $\Delta y\approx dy$.

Used to estimate values like $\sqrt{25.3}$ by linearising around $x=25$: $\sqrt{25.3}\approx 5+\dfrac{0.3}{10}=5.03.$

sections.heading: 6.5 Maxima and Minima
sections.body:
$c$ is a point of local maximum if $f(c)\geq f(x)$ for $x$ in a neighbourhood; local minimum if $f(c)\leq f(x)$ similarly.

Critical points: where $f'(c)=0$ or $f'(c)$ does not exist.

First-derivative test. Examine sign of $f'$ on either side of $c$:

  • $+\to-$: local max.
  • $-\to+$: local min.
  • No sign change: neither (point of inflection candidate).

Second-derivative test. If $f'(c)=0$ then: $f''(c)<0\Rightarrow$ local max; $f''(c)>0\Rightarrow$ local min; $f''(c)=0\Rightarrow$ test inconclusive (use first-derivative test).

sections.heading: 6.6 Absolute Maximum/Minimum on $[a,b]$
sections.body:
A continuous function on a closed bounded interval $[a,b]$ attains both an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum (Extreme Value Theorem).

Procedure.

  1. Find all critical points of $f$ in $(a,b)$.
  2. Compute $f$ at every critical point and at the endpoints $a$, $b$.
  3. The greatest of these is the absolute maximum; the smallest is the absolute minimum.

sections.heading: 6.7 Optimisation Problems
sections.body:
Real-world maxima/minima reduce to:

  1. Translate the problem into a function $f$ of a single variable, with constraints.
  2. Identify the feasible interval.
  3. Find $f'$, set $=0$, solve.
  4. Use first or second derivative test to confirm max/min.
  5. Interpret in original units.

Common archetypes: largest area for fixed perimeter, smallest surface area for fixed volume of a cylinder ($r=h/2$ for closed cylinder), shortest distance from a point to a curve, fence-along-a-river problems.
keyFormulae: Reduce to one variable using constraint, then $f'=0$

summary: Derivatives encode rates of change, slopes of tangents/normals, and behaviour of functions. Sign of $f'$ governs monotonicity; vanishing $f'$ flags critical points where local extrema may live, classified by the first or second derivative test. On a closed interval the absolute max/min are found among critical points and endpoints. Differentials give linear approximations $\Delta y\approx f'(x)\Delta x$ used for fast estimates and error analysis.
pitfalls: Confusing local extrema with absolute extrema.
Forgetting endpoints when computing absolute max/min on $[a,b]$.
Concluding nothing when $f''(c)=0$ — must fall back on first-derivative test.
Not checking whether the optimum from $f'=0$ lies in the feasible region.
Mixing up tangent and normal slopes — they are negative reciprocals.
